title: How Cardano Governance Works
description: A guided tour through Cardano's on-chain governance system, from the basics of who participates to how decisions are made and enacted.

stops.title: The Big Picture
stops.narrative:
Cardano governance is a tricameral system — three separate bodies share the power to approve changes to the protocol. No single entity controls anything. This was introduced by [CIP-1694](node:cip-1694) as part of Cardano's Voltaire phase, making it one of the most decentralized governance systems in blockchain.

The three bodies you see connected here are [DReps](node:drep) (representing ada holders), [SPOs](node:spo) (infrastructure operators), and the [Constitutional Committee](node:cc) (constitutional guardians). Every [governance action](node:governance-action) needs approval from at least two of these three.

stops.title: It Starts With You
stops.narrative:
Every ada holder has skin in the game. You can participate in governance in several ways: delegate your voting power to a [DRep](node:drep) you trust, register as a DRep yourself to vote directly, delegate to the pre-defined [Abstain](node:abstain-option) or [No Confidence](node:no-confidence-option) options, or even submit [governance actions](node:governance-action) (with a deposit).

Notice the two separate delegations flowing from here: one to DReps (for voting) and one to [SPOs](node:spo) (for block production). These are independent choices — you pick who votes on your behalf and who runs the infrastructure separately.

stops.title: Delegated Representatives
stops.narrative:
DReps are the democratic heart of the system. Any [ada holder](node:ada-holder) can register as one (500 ada deposit, refundable). They vote on ALL seven types of [governance actions](node:governance-action) — the broadest mandate of any governance body.

Their voting power equals the total lovelace delegated to them. One lovelace = one vote. This is [liquid democracy](node:liquid-democracy): you can change your delegation at any time, and it takes effect immediately. If your DRep votes against your wishes, switch to someone else — no waiting period.

DReps must stay active. If they don't vote within 20 epochs (~100 days), they become inactive and their delegated stake no longer counts.

stops.title: The Infrastructure Voice
stops.narrative:
Stake Pool Operators run the nodes that produce blocks and validate transactions. They got a new governance role in [Conway](node:conway-era): voting on matters that directly affect network operations.

SPOs vote on a focused subset: [Hard Forks](node:action-hard-fork) (they must upgrade their software), [No-Confidence](node:action-no-confidence) motions (a broken [CC](node:cc) could block upgrades), [Committee Updates](node:action-update-committee), [security-critical parameter changes](node:security-params), and [Info actions](node:action-info). They don't vote on treasury withdrawals or non-security parameter changes — those are policy decisions, not infrastructure ones.

Their voting weight comes from the stake delegated to their pools — the same stake that determines block production.

stops.title: Constitutional Guardians
stops.narrative:
The Constitutional Committee is unlike the other two bodies. Its members use one-member-one-vote (not stake-weighted), and its job is purely to check constitutionality — not to judge whether a proposal is a good idea.

Think of it as a constitutional court. The CC reviews [governance actions](node:governance-action) against the Cardano [Constitution](node:constitution) and votes on whether they comply. When CC members publish their vote rationale, they must cite specific constitutional articles.

The CC can be put into a 'no-confidence' state by the other two bodies, which blocks it from participating until a new committee is elected. This is the system's safety valve against a captured or dysfunctional CC.

stops.title: Governance Actions: The Currency of Change
stops.narrative:
Everything that changes in Cardano governance happens through governance actions — on-chain proposals submitted by any [ada holder](node:ada-holder). There are seven types, and each has different voting requirements.

Submitting one costs a refundable [deposit](node:deposit-mechanism) of 100,000 ada (the govActionDeposit). This isn't a fee — it's anti-spam. You get it back whether the action passes or expires. Each action lives for 6 epochs (~30 days) before it either gets ratified or expires.

From here, you can see the seven action types branching out, plus the lifecycle: [voting](node:voting) → [ratification](node:ratification) → [enactment](node:enactment).

stops.title: The Only Unanimous Action
stops.narrative:
Hard Fork Initiation is special — it's the only [governance action](node:governance-action) that requires approval from ALL THREE bodies: [CC](node:cc) at 2/3 majority, [DReps](node:drep) at 60%, and [SPOs](node:spo) at 51%.

Why? Because a hard fork is a non-backwards-compatible protocol upgrade. Everyone has to agree: the constitutional guardians (it's legal), the community representatives (it's wanted), and the node operators (they'll actually implement it). The [guardrails](node:guardrails-script) even require that 85% of stake pools have already upgraded their software before enactment.

A ratified hard fork also freezes everything else — all other pending governance actions are delayed until after the fork is enacted.

stops.title: Tuning the Protocol
stops.narrative:
Protocol Parameter Changes are the most common [governance action](node:governance-action) type. They modify the ~30 tunable parameters that control how Cardano works: block sizes, transaction fees, Plutus execution costs, staking rewards, and governance rules themselves.

Parameters are grouped into four categories, each with different voting thresholds. [Network](node:param-group-network), [Economic](node:param-group-economic), and [Technical](node:param-group-technical) groups need [DRep](node:drep) 67% + [CC](node:cc) 2/3. The [Governance group](node:param-group-governance) — parameters that control governance itself — needs the higher DRep 75% + CC 2/3, because changing the rules of the game is more consequential.

Some parameters are classified as '[security-relevant](node:security-params)' and additionally require [SPO](node:spo) approval, giving node operators a veto over changes that could destabilize the network.

stops.title: Why Smart Contract Costs Change
stops.narrative:
Plutus cost models are a specific protocol parameter that determines how much each smart contract operation costs. They're part of the [Technical group](node:param-group-technical).

The Plutus team benchmarks every builtin function on a reference machine, fits cost curves via R scripts, and the resulting coefficients become protocol parameters. When benchmarks show that certain operations are underpriced (like the recent +75% increase for integer division), the cost model gets updated through the same governance process as any other [parameter change](node:action-param-change).

These values are consensus-critical — every node must compute identical costs. The governance process ensures the community agrees before changing what developers pay to run smart contracts.

stops.title: How Decisions Get Made
stops.narrative:
Ratification happens at epoch boundaries — roughly every 5 days. At each boundary, the system recalculates the vote tally using the current stake snapshot. An action can become ratified even without new votes if delegation changes shift the stake distribution.

Votes are Yes, No, or Abstain. Registered stake that didn't vote counts as 'No'. Unregistered stake counts as 'Abstain'. This means apathy is not neutral — if you're registered and don't vote, you're effectively voting against everything.

Once all required thresholds are simultaneously met, the action is ratified. [Enactment](node:enactment) happens at the NEXT epoch boundary, giving the network a buffer to prepare.

stops.title: The Foundation
stops.narrative:
The Cardano Constitution is an off-chain document whose hash is recorded on-chain. It was ratified at the Buenos Aires Constitutional Convention in December 2024 after 63 workshops across 51 countries, with 95% delegate approval.

It contains 10 Tenets (core principles like transaction freedom and the 45 billion ada cap), Articles covering governance procedures, and Appendix I with permitted parameter ranges. The [CC](node:cc) evaluates every [governance action](node:governance-action) against this document.

The Constitution's enforceable rules are codified in the [Guardrails Script](node:guardrails-script) — an on-chain Plutus script that automatically rejects parameter changes or treasury withdrawals that violate constitutional bounds. Human judgment (CC) plus automated enforcement (script) together protect the system.

stops.title: Following the Money
stops.narrative:
Treasury withdrawals follow a multi-step process. First, a Net Change Limit must be set via an [Info Action](node:action-info) ([DRep](node:drep) 50% + [CC](node:cc) 2/3) — this caps how much can be withdrawn in a period. Then individual Budget proposals are approved. Only after both are in place can actual [Treasury Withdrawal](node:action-treasury) actions be submitted.

This layered process prevents unchecked spending. The treasury is funded by transaction fees and monetary expansion, so it's community money. The [Constitution](node:constitution) requires that withdrawals specify purpose, expected costs, and provide for auditable accounts.

The [Guardrails Script](node:guardrails-script) enforces the withdrawal limits on-chain, providing automated protection alongside the governance vote.
